project.name: next101
project.description: Next.js monorepo admin application with a Hono API service, Prisma data layer, shared permissions package, and Next.js admin UI. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: Project Orientation
tour.description: Start with the repository overview and working rules so the rest of the tour has context. The README gives the default Next.js startup path, while the agent instructions explain the actual monorepo shape, service conventions, frontend API rules, and database workflow that govern day-to-day changes.
tour.languageLesson: Markdown documentation in this project is not just prose; it acts as an operational contract for commands, architecture boundaries, and coding conventions.

tour.title: Workspace Workflow
tour.description: Next, look at the workspace configuration that turns the repository into a pnpm monorepo. The root package scripts coordinate app development, builds, Biome checks, and Prisma tasks, while the workspace file defines the apps and packages that participate in those commands.
tour.languageLesson: A pnpm workspace lets packages depend on each other by name while sharing one lockfile, which keeps app, service, and shared contracts synchronized during local development.

tour.title: Frontend Shell
tour.description: The admin application begins with the Next.js app layout and global styling. These files establish the root rendering shell, application-wide CSS, and the logged-in layout that wraps management pages before individual features render inside it.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router layouts compose hierarchically, so a root layout can provide global structure while route-group layouts add authenticated admin chrome around selected pages.

tour.title: Admin Navigation
tour.description: Building on the frontend shell, the route and layout components define how users land in the application and move through available menus. The home route redirects to the first accessible menu, while the frame and sidebar components provide the reusable navigation structure around feature pages.

tour.title: Frontend API Access
tour.description: Once the UI shell is clear, study how frontend code talks to the backend without raw fetch calls. The API barrel and Hono RPC client expose typed service calls to pages and components, and the utility wrapper centralizes success typing, error extraction, and feedback handling.
tour.languageLesson: Hono RPC uses the backend route type as a TypeScript contract, so frontend calls can be checked against server-defined paths, parameters, and JSON bodies.

tour.title: Next API Bridge
tour.description: The admin app also hosts the service through a Next.js route handler. This bridge mounts the Hono app under the Next API route, letting the frontend and backend live in the same deployed application while preserving the service's /api contract.
tour.languageLesson: A catch-all route such as [[...route]] lets Next.js forward many URL shapes to one handler, which is useful when another router owns the nested API surface.

tour.title: Service Route Tree
tour.description: Inside the service, the route index composes authentication, administration, organization, application, menu, role, upload, logging, and system routes on a shared OpenAPIHono router. This is the backend equivalent of the frontend shell: it shows which resource modules exist and how the public API is assembled.

tour.title: Resource Module Pattern
tour.description: The topology analysis highlighted the application route module as a strong code entry point, so use it as the representative resource pattern. Its index gathers individual endpoint files, the list handler shows request/response behavior, and the schema file defines the OpenAPI-backed Zod contracts reused by the module.
tour.languageLesson: Zod OpenAPI schemas serve two roles here: runtime validation for incoming data and generated TypeScript types for safer handler and client code.

tour.title: Data Model
tour.description: After seeing route handlers, move down to the database layer that backs those operations. The Prisma schema defines authentication, organizations, uploads, applications, menus, roles, assignments, system config, and operation logs, while the database client and migrations connect that model to PostgreSQL history.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma schema files describe the desired data model, while migrations record ordered SQL changes that make real databases match that model over time.

tour.title: Shared Contracts
tour.description: Finish with the shared package because it explains how frontend and backend agree on permissions and role/user flags. These contracts prevent each side of the monorepo from inventing separate meanings for the same authorization concepts.
tour.languageLesson: A shared TypeScript package is most valuable when it exposes stable domain vocabulary rather than duplicating implementation details from either the UI or service.
